label: Meat Breakdown & Yield
description: Calculator for breaking whole animals or primals into cuts, estimating yield, and routing cuts into freezer/storehouse and meal planning.

inputs.description: Animal and carcass data, preferred cut plan, and trimming/fat/bone preferences.

outputs.description: Detailed cut list with weights, percentages, recommended storage locations, and label data for printing and inventory logging.

nowButton.label: Plan Meat Breakdown Session Now
nowButton.tooltip: Turn this carcass or primal into labeled cuts, freezer loads, and meal planning in one guided session.

steps.title: Confirm animal and carcass details
steps.desc: Verify animal type, carcass weight, and any special notes (grass-fed, age, hanging time) so yield estimates are realistic.

metadata.cueNotes: This is a planning and prep step. Make sure scales, knives, and packaging supplies are ready.

steps.title: Plan cut list and destinations
steps.desc: Choose which primals, roasts, steaks, ground, bones, and offal you want, and assign each to freezer sections or storehouse destinations.

metadata.cueNotes: Focus on how your household really eats: roasts vs steaks, ground vs stew meat, bones for broth, etc.

steps.title: Estimate yields and packaging
steps.desc: Review estimated yields for each cut, adjust based on your trim preferences, and decide how many packages of each size to prepare.

metadata.cueNotes: This step sets expectations for the physical work session and freezer space required.

steps.title: Generate labels and inventory entries
steps.desc: Print or preview labels and create draft entries for freezer and storehouse inventory so you can log everything as you cut and package.

metadata.cueNotes: This step prepares the paperwork and digital tracking so nothing is lost when you’re busy cutting.

integration.notes: This node is the bridge between animal intake/butchery and long-term storehouse & freezer planning. Outputs from meat breakdown can be analyzed for cost-per-serving, Black hair nutrition, and preservation timing, then scheduled as sessions with SessionRunner.
